---
title: "How to speed up your STAN model compilation times"
author: Package Build
date: '2026-05-24'
slug: stan-compile-speedup
categories:
  - Blog
tags:
  - R
  - STAN
subtitle: ''
summary: "Some tips to help speedup STAN model compilation times."
authors: []
lastmod: '2026-05-24T06:00:00+00:00'
featured: false
image:
  caption: ''
  focal_point: 'Center'
  preview_only: false
  alt_text: '...'
projects: []
toc: true
---

## Introduction

In my previous job my work computer was a Windows desktop -- yes the days before laptops and hot-desking!

My PhD student was interested in Bayesian methods so we put together an R package which included some STAN models. I was always frustrated how slowly these compiled on our Windows machines.

When I returned to the Apple kingdom I was shocked how much faster they compiled.

Here are the compilation times for the models in our [mrbayes](https://okezie94.github.io/mrbayes/) package.

* Windows 11: seconds
* Ubuntu 24.04 through WSL on the same machine: seconds
* M4 MacBook Air: 73 seconds

## Tip 1: Disabling debug builds essentially makes no difference

I had wondered whether running a non-debug compilation with say

```r
pkgbuild::compile_dll(debug = FALSE)
```

would speed things up. It turns out it does not.

## Tip 1: Windows users should use R through Windows Subsystem for Linux

The compilers on Windows are very bad a coping with STAN's templating.

On Linux we can use clang.

## Tip 2: Enable parallel compilations

In your _~/.R/Makevars_ file set

```sh
MAKEFLAGS = -j8
```

replacing 8 with some appropriate number for your machine. This sets how many models can be compiled in parallel.

Enabling this on my M4 MacBook Air reduces the compilation time to 36 seconds.

## Tip 3: Enable ccache

Download ccache, e.g., on macOS

```sh
brew install ccache
```

In your _~/.R/Makevars_ file set

```sh
CC = ccache clang
CXX = ccache clang++
CXX17 = ccache clang++
```

On M4 MacBook Air, the second compilation of the package when the STAN code is unchanged only takes 4 seconds.

<!--
<img src="/post/2026/runiverse-tips/img/twosamplemr-dependencies-clicked.png" alt="Screenshot of expanding the dependencies pill on an R-universe package page." width="730" style="display: block; margin: auto;">
-->

## Summary

In summary, I have shown how to
